text:
I was using the <https://github.com/r-devel/r-svn/pull/228|r-devel/r-svn to create a patch>, but it is <https://github.com/r-devel/r-svn/actions/runs/21313120506/job/61352198199?pr=228|failing the CI> and I wanted to check it locally. But I don't get to build R from that repo as the build step assumes it is on a svn repository:
```R is now configured for x86_64-pc-linux-gnu

  Source directory:            .
  Installation directory:      /home/lluis/bin/R/vp

  C compiler:                  gcc -std=gnu2x  -g -O2
  Fortran fixed-form compiler: gfortran  -g -O2

  Default C++ compiler:        g++ -std=gnu++17  -g -O2
  Fortran free-form compiler:  gfortran  -g -O2
  Obj-C compiler:	        

  Interfaces supported:        X11, tcltk
  External libraries:          pcre2, readline, BLAS(OpenBLAS), curl, libdeflate
  Additional capabilities:     PNG, JPEG, TIFF, NLS, cairo, ICU
  Options enabled:             shared R library, shared BLAS, R profiling, memory profiling, libdeflate for lazyload

  Capabilities skipped:        
  Options not enabled:         

  Recommended packages:        yes

 $ R/r-svn: make -j4
ERROR: not an svn checkout
make: *** [Makefile:107: svnonly] Error 1```

text: I can create a patch and move to the svn local repo to test it but I was wondering if someone has found a way to build it locally on the git repo. Thanks!

elements.text: I can create a patch and move to the svn local repo to test it but I was wondering if someone has found a way to build it locally on the git repo. Thanks!

text:
great Q I am always copying the patch over manually but it's somewhat tedious, would be smart to get this working instead

the answer must be in the .github folder which gives the recipe for how GHA does it.

at a glance it must be this script

<https://github.com/r-devel/r-svn/blob/main/.github/scripts/svn-info.sh|https://github.com/r-devel/r-svn/blob/main/.github/scripts/svn-info.sh>

text: There is this absurd limitation that errors if the build isn't from a proper svn thing, with the revision that is hard coded. Whenever I work with r build, I immediately delete this limit, just so I can get on with the real work.
